sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale (RASS) is a 10-point observational scale ranging from +4 (combative) to -5 (unarousable). It helps describe agitation or sedation severity and can support monitoring, documentation, and reassessment, but it does not by itself determine whether sedation is required.

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- RASS is a descriptive tool, not a stand-alone treatment trigger
- Sedation decisions remain based on clinical judgement, immediate risk to the patient or others, and local scope or protocol
- Reassess and document the score before and after any de-escalation, restraint, sedation, or deterioration in conscious state

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Use RASS to describe agitation or sedation severity when behavioural disturbance or reduced conscious state is present
- Document the score alongside the clinical context, observed risks, and other assessment findings
- Do not delay safety measures, de-escalation, or urgent transport while trying to complete the tool perfectly

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Use serial RASS scoring to monitor response to sedation, restraint release, deterioration, or recovery
- Combine RASS with airway, respiratory, circulatory, and temperature assessment rather than relying on it in isolation
- Manage the patient under the relevant behavioural disturbance, intoxication, or unconsciousness guideline rather than as a stand-alone pathway
